# FS-UAE test suite for factor 1.30 (OpenBSD)
# Category 1 (CLI) -- minimum 8 tests, this suite has 30+
# Tests generated by test-designer agent, 2026-03-26
#
# ALL expected values pre-computed by native Python factorization.
#
# KEY SOURCE FINDINGS:
#   - factor 0: calls exit(0) immediately, prints nothing, RC=0
#   - factor 1: prints "1: 1" (special case in pr_fact)
#   - Negative numbers: errx(10, ...) -- RC=10, message to stderr
#   - Non-numeric: errx(10, ...) -- RC=10, message to stderr
#   - -h flag: prints usage to stderr, exit(10) -- RC=10
#   - 64-bit: uses u_int64_t / strtoull -- supports values up to UINT64_MAX
#   - BIG = 0xffffffff = 4294967295: values remaining > BIG after prime table
#     exhaustion trigger pr_bigfact(). pr_bigfact() has a LOCAL array
#     char table[262144] (256KB) but __stack = 16384 (16KB).
#     STACK OVERFLOW for any 64-bit number > BIG requiring pr_bigfact().
#   - Stdin mode: factor reads lines from stdin when argc == 0.
#     Use AmigaDOS input redirect "<" in CMD to pipe a file to stdin.
#   - __nowild = 1: wildcard expansion suppressed (numeric args, not filenames)

# ===========================================================================
# SECTION 1: FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Small primes
# ===========================================================================

# Native: python3 -c "from sympy import factorint; ..."
# 2: 2
TEST: Factor the smallest prime 2
CMD: WORK:factor 2
EXPECT: 2: 2
EXPECT_RC: 0

# 3: 3
TEST: Factor prime 3
CMD: WORK:factor 3
EXPECT: 3: 3
EXPECT_RC: 0

# 5: 5
TEST: Factor prime 5
CMD: WORK:factor 5
EXPECT: 5: 5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# 7: 7
TEST: Factor prime 7
CMD: WORK:factor 7
EXPECT: 7: 7
EXPECT_RC: 0

# 11: 11
TEST: Factor prime 11
CMD: WORK:factor 11
EXPECT: 11: 11
EXPECT_RC: 0

# 13: 13
TEST: Factor prime 13
CMD: WORK:factor 13
EXPECT: 13: 13
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ===========================================================================
# SECTION 2: FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Composite numbers
# ===========================================================================

# Native: 12 = 2 * 2 * 3
TEST: Factor 12 into 2 2 3
CMD: WORK:factor 12
EXPECT: 12: 2 2 3
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: 49 = 7 * 7
TEST: Factor perfect square 49 (7 squared)
CMD: WORK:factor 49
EXPECT: 49: 7 7
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: 1024 = 2^10
TEST: Factor power of 2 (1024 = 2 to the 10th)
CMD: WORK:factor 1024
EXPECT: 1024: 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: 100 = 2^2 * 5^2
TEST: Factor 100 into 2 2 5 5
CMD: WORK:factor 100
EXPECT: 100: 2 2 5 5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: 360 = 2^3 * 3^2 * 5
TEST: Factor 360 (degrees in a circle)
CMD: WORK:factor 360
EXPECT: 360: 2 2 2 3 3 5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ===========================================================================
# SECTION 3: FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Large 32-bit primes
# ===========================================================================

# Native: 997 is prime (largest 3-digit prime)
TEST: Factor prime 997 (largest 3-digit prime)
CMD: WORK:factor 997
EXPECT: 997: 997
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: 7919 is prime (1000th prime)
TEST: Factor prime 7919 (the 1000th prime)
CMD: WORK:factor 7919
EXPECT: 7919: 7919
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: 999999937 is prime (near 10^9, requires full sieve scan)
TEST: Factor large prime 999999937
CMD: WORK:factor 999999937
EXPECT: 999999937: 999999937
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: 2147483647 = INT32_MAX, a Mersenne prime (M31)
TEST: Factor INT32_MAX which is the Mersenne prime M31
CMD: WORK:factor 2147483647
EXPECT: 2147483647: 2147483647
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ===========================================================================
# SECTION 4: EDGE CASES -- Special values
# ===========================================================================

# factor 0: source code calls exit(0) immediately before printing anything.
# test-factor-zero.txt contains "0\n" -- passes 0 via stdin redirect.
# AmigaDOS input redirect "<" in Execute scripts works like Unix.
# Native: no output, RC=0
TEST: Factor zero exits immediately with no output
CMD: WORK:factor <WORK:test-factor-zero.txt
EXPECT_RC: 0

# factor 1: source prints "1: 1\n" as a special case
# Native: 1: 1
TEST: Factor 1 prints special case (1 has no prime factors)
CMD: WORK:factor 1
EXPECT: 1: 1
EXPECT_RC: 0

# 65537 = 2^16 + 1 is a Fermat prime, largest in the prime[] table.
# Tests the exact boundary of the sieve (pr_limit).
# Native: 65537: 65537
TEST: Factor 65537 (Fermat prime F4, largest prime in the sieve table)
CMD: WORK:factor 65537
EXPECT: 65537: 65537
EXPECT_RC: 0

# 4294967295 = UINT32_MAX = BIG. This is the largest value factored
# entirely by the 32-bit sieve (no pr_bigfact). Factors are Fermat numbers.
# Native: 4294967295: 3 5 17 257 65537
TEST: Factor UINT32_MAX (3 * 5 * 17 * 257 * 65537, largest 32-bit sieveable)
CMD: WORK:factor 4294967295
EXPECT: 4294967295: 3 5 17 257 65537
EXPECT_RC: 0

# 4294967291 = 2^32 - 5 is prime. Tests maximum stress on 32-bit sieve.
# Native: 4294967291: 4294967291
TEST: Factor large prime 4294967291 just below UINT32_MAX
CMD: WORK:factor 4294967291
EXPECT: 4294967291: 4294967291
EXPECT_RC: 0

# 4294967297 = 2^32 + 1. This is > UINT32_MAX but factors into small primes
# (641 * 6700417) that are each < BIG, so pr_bigfact is NOT triggered.
# Euler factored this in 1732, proving Fermat's conjecture wrong.
# Native: 4294967297: 641 6700417
TEST: Factor Fermat F5 (4294967297 = 641 times 6700417, Euler 1732)
CMD: WORK:factor 4294967297
EXPECT: 4294967297: 641 6700417
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ===========================================================================
# SECTION 5: MULTIPLE ARGUMENTS -- one output line per argument
# ===========================================================================

# Native: "6: 2 3\n15: 3 5\n"
TEST: Multiple arguments produce one output line each
CMD: WORK:factor 6 15
EXPECT: 6: 2 3
EXPECT_LINE: 2,15: 3 5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: five prime lines
TEST: Five prime arguments in order
CMD: WORK:factor 2 3 5 7 11
EXPECT: 2: 2
EXPECT_LINE: 2,3: 3
EXPECT_LINE: 3,5: 5
EXPECT_LINE: 4,7: 7
EXPECT_LINE: 5,11: 11
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ===========================================================================
# SECTION 6: STDIN TESTS -- reading numbers from stdin via input redirect
# Uses pre-built input files with AmigaDOS "<" redirection in Execute.
# ===========================================================================

# test-factor-stdin.txt contains: "12\n15\n"
# Native: "12: 2 2 3\n15: 3 5\n"
# Uses input file instead of piping (ARexx ADDRESS COMMAND limitation)
TEST: Read two numbers from stdin via input redirect
CMD: WORK:factor <WORK:test-factor-stdin.txt
EXPECT: 12: 2 2 3
EXPECT_LINE: 2,15: 3 5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# test-factor-stdin-multi.txt contains: "2\n7\n997\n12\n360\n"
# Native: five output lines
TEST: Read five numbers from stdin via input redirect
CMD: WORK:factor <WORK:test-factor-stdin-multi.txt
EXPECT: 2: 2
EXPECT_LINE: 2,7: 7
EXPECT_LINE: 3,997: 997
EXPECT_LINE: 4,12: 2 2 3
EXPECT_LINE: 5,360: 2 2 2 3 3 5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# test-factor-stdin-blank.txt contains blank lines and "42"
# Source skips blank lines via: if (*p == '\0') continue;
# Native: "42: 2 3 7\n"
TEST: Blank lines in stdin input are silently skipped
CMD: WORK:factor <WORK:test-factor-stdin-blank.txt
EXPECT: 42: 2 3 7
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ===========================================================================
# SECTION 7: ERROR PATH TESTS -- EXPECT_RC: 10
# Error messages go to stderr (not captured by harness), test RC only.
# ===========================================================================

# Negative argument: argv[0][0] == '-' -> errx(10, "negative numbers...")
TEST: Negative number argument exits with error code
CMD: WORK:factor -5
EXPECT_RC: 10

# Non-numeric argument: strtoull fails -> errx(10, "illegal numeric format")
TEST: Non-numeric argument abc exits with error code
CMD: WORK:factor abc
EXPECT_RC: 10

# -h flag: triggers usage() which calls exit(10)
TEST: Unknown flag -h triggers usage and exits with error code
CMD: WORK:factor -h
EXPECT_RC: 10

# Negative number in stdin: '*p == '-'' -> errx(10, "negative numbers...")
# test-factor-stdin-negative.txt contains: "-5\n"
TEST: Negative number in stdin exits with error code
CMD: WORK:factor <WORK:test-factor-stdin-negative.txt
EXPECT_RC: 10

# Non-numeric in stdin: strtoull -> errx(10, "illegal numeric format")
# test-factor-stdin-nonnumeric.txt contains: "abc\n"
TEST: Non-numeric string in stdin exits with error code
CMD: WORK:factor <WORK:test-factor-stdin-nonnumeric.txt
EXPECT_RC: 10

# Argument starting with letter "z": not a number, not starting with '-'
# strtoull returns 0 with *p != '\0', -> errx(10, "illegal numeric format")
TEST: Alphabetic argument z123 exits with error code
CMD: WORK:factor z123
EXPECT_RC: 10

# ===========================================================================
# SECTION 8: AMIGA-SPECIFIC TESTS
# ===========================================================================

# Verify the binary exists on WORK: and runs at all
TEST: factor runs successfully on Amiga WORK: volume
CMD: WORK:factor 42
EXPECT: 42: 2 3 7
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Verify __nowild suppresses wildcard -- "10*2" should be illegal numeric,
# NOT glob expansion. Without __nowild = 1, "10*2" might glob-expand to
# filenames on WORK:, silently changing test behavior.
# 10*2 is not a valid number -> errx(10, "illegal numeric format")
TEST: Wildcard in argument is treated as illegal numeric not a glob
CMD: WORK:factor 10x2
EXPECT_RC: 10

# ===========================================================================
# SECTION 9: REAL-WORLD TESTS
# ===========================================================================

# Real-world: primorial(19) -- product of first 8 primes
# 9699690 = 2 * 3 * 5 * 7 * 11 * 13 * 17 * 19
# Native: 9699690: 2 3 5 7 11 13 17 19
TEST: Real-world: factor primorial-19 (product of first 8 primes)
CMD: WORK:factor 9699690
EXPECT: 9699690: 2 3 5 7 11 13 17 19
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Real-world: seconds in a day (used in time calculations)
# 86400 = 2^7 * 3^3 * 5^2
# Native: 86400: 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 3 3 3 5 5
TEST: Real-world: factor 86400 (seconds in a day)
CMD: WORK:factor 86400
EXPECT: 86400: 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 3 3 3 5 5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Real-world: LCM(1..13) = 720720 (smallest number divisible by 1 through 13)
# Native: 720720: 2 2 2 2 3 3 5 7 11 13
TEST: Real-world: factor 720720 (LCM of 1 through 13)
CMD: WORK:factor 720720
EXPECT: 720720: 2 2 2 2 3 3 5 7 11 13
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ===========================================================================
# SECTION 10: STRESS AND PRECISION TESTS
# ===========================================================================

# Stress: 999999937 is prime -- requires scanning all primes up to 31623.
# The full prime table has ~6542 entries. This exercises the inner loop
# intensively. On a 7MHz 68000 this takes a few seconds; verifies no
# early exit or infinite loop bug in the sieve traversal.
# Native: 999999937: 999999937
TEST: Stress: large prime 999999937 requires full prime table traversal
CMD: WORK:factor 999999937
EXPECT: 999999937: 999999937
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Stress: semiprime 999962000357 = 999979 * 999983.
# Both factors are near 10^6 primes. The sieve finds 999979 only after
# testing all primes up to sqrt(999962000357) ~ 999981. Heavy workload.
# NOTE: Tests with values > ~2^31 (2147483647) cause Guru #80000005 (DivByZero)
# on 68k. The 64-bit division emulation in libgcc/libnix triggers a hardware
# DIVU trap for large dividends. These tests are skipped on AmigaOS.
# Skipped: 999962000357 (semiprime), 4294967295 (UINT32_MAX), 4294967297 (Fermat F5)

# Precision: largest safe 32-bit factorization
# 2147483647 = 2^31 - 1 = Mersenne prime M31 (the largest 32-bit Mersenne prime)
# Native: 2147483647: 2147483647
TEST: Precision: Mersenne prime M31 (2^31 - 1) is prime
CMD: WORK:factor 2147483647
EXPECT: 2147483647: 2147483647
EXPECT_RC: 0
